paragraphs.label: HKSAR v Lam Wai Keung s.3
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Under s.3 of the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169), it is an offence to cruelly beat, kick, ill-treat, torture, or cause unnecessary suffering to any animal. Deliberately stabbing or injuring an animal falls squarely within this provision. The maximum penalty is a fine of HK$200,000 and 3 years' imprisonment.
paragraphs.text_private: Under s.3 of the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169), it is an offence to cruelly beat, kick, ill-treat, torture, or cause unnecessary suffering to any animal. Deliberately stabbing or injuring an animal falls squarely within this provision. The maximum penalty is a fine of HK$200,000 and 3 years' imprisonment.

paragraphs.label: HKSAR v Lam Wai Keung s.4
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Section 4 of Cap. 169 makes it an offence to abandon an animal in circumstances likely to cause it unnecessary suffering. Owners have a positive duty to ensure their animals are not subjected to unnecessary pain or distress.
paragraphs.text_private: Section 4 of Cap. 169 makes it an offence to abandon an animal in circumstances likely to cause it unnecessary suffering. Owners have a positive duty to ensure their animals are not subjected to unnecessary pain or distress.

propositions.label: Cruelty to animals — s.3 Cap. 169
propositions.label_zh: 虐待動物 — 第169章第3條
propositions.statement_en: Under s.3 of the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance (Cap. 169), it is an offence to cruelly beat, kick, ill-treat, torture, or cause unnecessary suffering to any animal. Deliberately stabbing or injuring an animal falls squarely within this provision. The maximum penalty is a fine of HK$200,000 and 3 years' imprisonment.
propositions.statement_zh: 根據《防止殘酷對待動物條例》（第169章）第3條，殘酷毆打、踢踹、虐待、折磨或對任何動物造成不必要痛苦屬刑事罪行。故意刺傷或傷害動物明確屬於此條文的規管範圍。最高刑罰為罰款港幣200,000元及監禁3年。

propositions.label: Abandonment and neglect — s.4 Cap. 169
propositions.label_zh: 遺棄及疏忽 — 第169章第4條
propositions.statement_en: Section 4 of Cap. 169 makes it an offence to abandon an animal in circumstances likely to cause it unnecessary suffering. Owners have a positive duty to ensure their animals are not subjected to unnecessary pain or distress.
propositions.statement_zh: 第169章第4條規定，在可能導致動物遭受不必要痛苦的情況下遺棄動物屬刑事罪行。動物主人有積極責任確保其動物不受不必要的痛苦或困擾。

paragraphs.label: HKSAR v Chan Siu Ming
paragraphs.public_excerpt: In sentencing for animal cruelty under Cap. 169, the court weighs the severity and duration of suffering, whether the act was deliberate, and whether the animal died. Deliberate stabbing or killing of a pet will ordinarily attract an immediate custodial sentence, with the starting point increasing with the degree of premeditation and cruelty.
paragraphs.text_private: In sentencing for animal cruelty under Cap. 169, the court weighs the severity and duration of suffering, whether the act was deliberate, and whether the animal died. Deliberate stabbing or killing of a pet will ordinarily attract an immediate custodial sentence, with the starting point increasing with the degree of premeditation and cruelty.

propositions.label: Sentencing factors for animal cruelty
propositions.label_zh: 動物虐待罪行的量刑因素
propositions.statement_en: In sentencing for animal cruelty under Cap. 169, the court weighs the severity and duration of suffering, whether the act was deliberate, and whether the animal died. Deliberate stabbing or killing of a pet will ordinarily attract an immediate custodial sentence, with the starting point increasing with the degree of premeditation and cruelty.
propositions.statement_zh: 就第169章動物虐待罪行量刑時，法院衡量痛苦的嚴重程度及持續時間、行為是否故意，以及動物是否死亡。故意刺傷或殺死寵物通常會被判處即時監禁，量刑起點隨預謀程度及殘酷程度而增加。

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 200 [30]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When sentencing for multiple offenses, the court must consider the overall criminality and ensure the total sentence is just and proportionate, not excessive.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 200 [30]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A timely guilty plea warrants a substantial reduction in the sentence that would otherwise be imposed.

paragraphs.label: [2023] HKDC 200 [30]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The court has discretion to order sentences for separate offenses to run either consecutively or concurrently, based on the need to reflect the overall criminal conduct.

propositions.label: Totality principle application
propositions.statement_en: When sentencing for multiple offenses, the court must consider the overall criminality and ensure the total sentence is just and proportionate, not excessive.

propositions.label: Guilty plea discount
propositions.statement_en: A timely guilty plea warrants a substantial reduction in the sentence that would otherwise be imposed.

propositions.label: Consecutive vs concurrent sentencing
propositions.statement_en: The court has discretion to order sentences for separate offenses to run either consecutively or concurrently, based on the need to reflect the overall criminal conduct.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKDC 55 [30]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When sentencing for multiple offenses, the court must consider the overall criminality and ensure the total sentence is just and proportionate, not excessive.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKDC 55 [30]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A timely guilty plea warrants a reduction in the sentence that would otherwise be imposed.

paragraphs.label: [2020] HKDC 55 [30]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The court has discretion to order sentences for separate offenses to run either consecutively or concurrently, based on the need to reflect the overall criminality.

propositions.label: Totality principle application
propositions.statement_en: When sentencing for multiple offenses, the court must consider the overall criminality and ensure the total sentence is just and proportionate, not excessive.

propositions.label: Guilty plea discount
propositions.statement_en: A timely guilty plea warrants a reduction in the sentence that would otherwise be imposed.

propositions.label: Consecutive vs concurrent sentences
propositions.statement_en: The court has discretion to order sentences for separate offenses to run either consecutively or concurrently, based on the need to reflect the overall criminality.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCFA 7 [23]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: An appeal to the Court of First Instance from a magistrate under section 113 of the Magistrates Ordinance is an appeal by way of rehearing on the evidence before the trial court, which may be supplemented by further evidence admitted under the court's statutory power.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCFA 7 [28], [35]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: On an appeal by way of rehearing under section 113, the appellate judge has a duty to come to their own independent conclusion on disputed issues of fact and law, including the ultimate question of whether guilt is proved beyond reasonable doubt.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCFA 7 [33], [35]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The 'plainly wrong' test for appellate interference with a trial court's findings of fact or credibility is not the appropriate standard of review for an appeal under section 113, as it is inconsistent with the appellate court's duty to reach its own conclusion.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCFA 7 [48]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Whether a judge has properly discharged the duty of rehearing is determined by examining the substance of the judgment's reasoning, not its form. The use of phrases like 'not in error' or 'plainly wrong' does not automatically indicate an incorrect standard was applied if the judgment shows the judge independently assessed the evidence.

paragraphs.label: [2024] HKCFA 7 [50]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: On a rehearing under section 113, the appellate judge must independently rehear contested issues of fact, including those involving inferences (such as dishonest intent), rather than merely reviewing the magistrate's decision.

propositions.label: Nature of appeal under s.113
propositions.statement_en: An appeal to the Court of First Instance from a magistrate under section 113 of the Magistrates Ordinance is an appeal by way of rehearing on the evidence before the trial court, which may be supplemented by further evidence admitted under the court's statutory power.

propositions.label: Appellate duty on rehearing
propositions.statement_en: On an appeal by way of rehearing under section 113, the appellate judge has a duty to come to their own independent conclusion on disputed issues of fact and law, including the ultimate question of whether guilt is proved beyond reasonable doubt.

propositions.label: Inapplicability of 'plainly wrong' test
propositions.statement_en: The 'plainly wrong' test for appellate interference with a trial court's findings of fact or credibility is not the appropriate standard of review for an appeal under section 113, as it is inconsistent with the appellate court's duty to reach its own conclusion.

propositions.label: Assessment of appellate duty discharge
propositions.statement_en: Whether a judge has properly discharged the duty of rehearing is determined by examining the substance of the judgment's reasoning, not its form. The use of phrases like 'not in error' or 'plainly wrong' does not automatically indicate an incorrect standard was applied if the judgment shows the judge independently assessed the evidence.

propositions.label: Appellate task on factual inference
propositions.statement_en: On a rehearing under section 113, the appellate judge must independently rehear contested issues of fact, including those involving inferences (such as dishonest intent), rather than merely reviewing the magistrate's decision.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 298 [34]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The specific intent to cause harm (such as with a knife) can be formed during the course of an incident, not necessarily before it begins. The court may infer intent from the defendant's actions and the time available for reflection between obtaining a weapon and using it.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 298 [34]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A court may infer a defendant's intent from their observable conduct and the sequence/timing of their actions, particularly the period between arming themselves and committing the violent act, which can indicate deliberation rather than impulsive loss of control.

propositions.label: Intent formation timing
propositions.statement_en: The specific intent to cause harm (such as with a knife) can be formed during the course of an incident, not necessarily before it begins. The court may infer intent from the defendant's actions and the time available for reflection between obtaining a weapon and using it.

propositions.label: Inference of intent from conduct
propositions.statement_en: A court may infer a defendant's intent from their observable conduct and the sequence/timing of their actions, particularly the period between arming themselves and committing the violent act, which can indicate deliberation rather than impulsive loss of control.

paragraphs.label: [2013] HKCA 169 [34]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The court may infer a defendant's intent to cause harm from the sequence and timing of events, particularly where there is a measurable delay between acquiring a weapon and using it, indicating an opportunity for reflection rather than an immediate, impulsive act.

paragraphs.label: [2013] HKCA 169 [34]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: A finding that a defendant had time to reflect between obtaining a weapon and using it can negate a defense of sudden provocation or loss of control, supporting a finding of deliberate intent.

propositions.label: Inference of intent from temporal sequence
propositions.label_en: Inference of intent from temporal sequence
propositions.statement_en: The court may infer a defendant's intent to cause harm from the sequence and timing of events, particularly where there is a measurable delay between acquiring a weapon and using it, indicating an opportunity for reflection rather than an immediate, impulsive act.

propositions.label: Reflection negates sudden provocation
propositions.statement_en: A finding that a defendant had time to reflect between obtaining a weapon and using it can negate a defense of sudden provocation or loss of control, supporting a finding of deliberate intent.

paragraphs.label: [2012] HKCA 247 [34]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The court may infer an intent to cause harm from the sequence and timing of events, particularly where there is a measurable delay between acquiring a weapon and using it, allowing for reflection.

paragraphs.label: [2012] HKCA 247 [34]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: An act is less likely to be considered a spontaneous reaction if there is a significant interval between obtaining the weapon and the assault, during which the defendant was not visibly overwhelmed by emotion.

propositions.label: Inference of intent from temporal sequence
propositions.label_en: Inference of intent from temporal sequence
propositions.statement_en: The court may infer an intent to cause harm from the sequence and timing of events, particularly where there is a measurable delay between acquiring a weapon and using it, allowing for reflection.

propositions.label: Absence of immediate provocation
propositions.statement_en: An act is less likely to be considered a spontaneous reaction if there is a significant interval between obtaining the weapon and the assault, during which the defendant was not visibly overwhelmed by emotion.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCA 157 [10]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The prosecution must prove the defendant's guilt beyond a reasonable doubt. If, after considering all evidence, a reasonable doubt exists as to the defendant's guilt, the prosecution has failed to meet its burden and the defendant must be found not guilty.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCA 157 [b]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: Manslaughter can be established by proving the defendant committed an unlawful and dangerous act. The act must be objectively dangerous in the eyes of all sober and reasonable people.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCA 157 [55]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When an experienced criminal practitioner, after considering all possible defences and based on their instructions, concludes that only certain defences are available and rejects others, the court will not interfere with that professional judgment unless it is shown to be unreasonable or improper.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKCA 157 [20]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: The fact that the prosecution calls certain individuals as witnesses indicates that they are not being held responsible for the crime, which can be relevant to negating an inference of conspiracy involving those individuals.

propositions.label: Standard of proof in criminal cases
propositions.label_en: Standard of proof in criminal cases
propositions.statement_en: The prosecution must prove the defendant's guilt beyond a reasonable doubt. If, after considering all evidence, a reasonable doubt exists as to the defendant's guilt, the prosecution has failed to meet its burden and the defendant must be found not guilty.

propositions.label: Manslaughter by unlawful and dangerous act
propositions.label_en: Manslaughter by unlawful and dangerous act
propositions.statement_en: Manslaughter can be established by proving the defendant committed an unlawful and dangerous act. The act must be objectively dangerous in the eyes of all sober and reasonable people.

propositions.label: Legal professional judgment on defences
propositions.statement_en: When an experienced criminal practitioner, after considering all possible defences and based on their instructions, concludes that only certain defences are available and rejects others, the court will not interfere with that professional judgment unless it is shown to be unreasonable or improper.

propositions.label: Conspiracy inference from witness status
propositions.statement_en: The fact that the prosecution calls certain individuals as witnesses indicates that they are not being held responsible for the crime, which can be relevant to negating an inference of conspiracy involving those individuals.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 184 [38]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When sentencing for multiple distinct offences arising from the same background, a court may order sentences to run partially consecutively, rather than fully concurrently or consecutively, to reflect the separate criminality.

paragraphs.label: [2021] HKDC 184 [38]
paragraphs.public_excerpt: When imposing consecutive sentences for multiple offences, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final aggregate sentence is not excessive and is proportionate to the offender's overall criminal conduct.

propositions.label: Partial consecutive sentencing for separate offences
propositions.label_en: Partial consecutive sentencing for separate offences
propositions.statement_en: When sentencing for multiple distinct offences arising from the same background, a court may order sentences to run partially consecutively, rather than fully concurrently or consecutively, to reflect the separate criminality.

propositions.label: Totality principle application
propositions.statement_en: When imposing consecutive sentences for multiple offences, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final aggregate sentence is not excessive and is proportionate to the offender's overall criminal conduct.

paragraphs.public_excerpt: When an offender is convicted of multiple distinct offences arising from the same incident, the court may impose partially consecutive sentences to reflect the separate criminality of each offence.

paragraphs.public_excerpt: When imposing consecutive sentences for multiple offences, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final combined sentence is not excessive and is proportionate to the overall criminality.

propositions.label: Application of totality principle
propositions.statement_en: When imposing consecutive sentences for multiple offences, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final combined sentence is not excessive and is proportionate to the overall criminality.

paragraphs.public_excerpt: When sentencing for multiple separate offences arising from the same background, a court may order sentences to run partially consecutively, applying the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence is just and appropriate.

paragraphs.public_excerpt: When sentencing for multiple separate offences arising from the same background incident, the court may impose a partially consecutive sentence, ordering only a portion of the sentence for one charge to run consecutively with another.
